trigger.reason: Claude Cowork expansion to mobile and web is a new product capability release from Anthropic that targets office productivity and coding workflows, likely to trigger sympathy-fade selling in low-code dev tools, customer-service software, and productivity SaaS.

primary_event.headline: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web
primary_event.summary: Anthropic announced an expansion of Claude Cowork, its coding agent, to mobile and web platforms, allowing users to start tasks on desktop and track progress across devices even when their primary machine is offline. This extends the reach of Anthropic's agent capabilities beyond the initial desktop-only release.

discovery.trigger_acknowledgment: Anthropic's Claude Cowork expansion to mobile/web — a coding-agent capability extension targeting productivity and coding workflows.
discovery.run_summary: The sympathy fade hit an oddly-shaped basket: semiconductor picks-and-shovels names (ONTO, AEIS, PLAB, AMBA, CGNX) got dragged on a coding-agent story that has essentially no business-model overlap with process control, photomasks, edge AI SoCs, or industrial machine vision. Two software/cloud names actually moved UP (PCTY, DOCN), so the sympathy-fade pattern is only visible on the semi-cap side. Best OVERDONE candidates are the semi-cap picks-and-shovels names where filings show AI capex is a tailwind, not a threat.

discoveries.name: Onto Innovation Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: Risk Factors describe Onto as a process-control/metrology/inspection supplier to semiconductor device manufacturers. AI is mentioned only as an internal operational tool ('use or anticipated use of new and evolving technologies, such as AI, by us or third parties may increase or create new operational risks'), not as a competitive threat to the business model. No language about coding agents or productivity software affecting demand.
discoveries.thesis: A coding-agent product from Anthropic has zero mechanical bearing on demand for advanced-node process control equipment. If anything, more AI chips (which is what Cowork-style demand ultimately drives) means more wafer starts at leading nodes, which means MORE metrology/inspection tools. The five-day drawdown makes this cheaper than a clean sympathy-only setup, but there's no filings-based reason to think the trigger event actually impairs the thesis.
discoveries.what_confirms: Semi-cap peers (KLAC, ACLS, AMAT) trading in-line without company-specific bad news; AI-related capex commentary from TSMC/Intel/Samsung continues to accelerate; Rigaku deal (per 10-Q) closes cleanly.
discoveries.what_kills: A leading-edge fab capex cut announcement, a WFE downgrade cycle from Applied/LAM/KLAC, or an ONTO-specific customer-loss 8-K within the fade window.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Semi-cap picks-and-shovels names get lumped into 'AI-adjacent selloff' baskets on AI application-layer news, even though the mechanical relationship is INVERSE — more AI apps → more chips → more capex → more tools. This is a repeatable pattern.
discoveries.catalyst: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: Included in trigger's blast radius purely because it's a technology-sector name that moved on the day; there is no product-market overlap with coding agents.
research_pointers: Check if 5-day drawdown is company-specific (Rigaku deal news, guidance cut) vs pure sympathy
Cross-check WFE peer moves (KLAC, AMAT, LRCX) to confirm sector-wide fade
Watch for TSMC monthly rev / capex commentary as the real fundamental driver

discoveries.name: Advanced Energy Industries, Inc
discoveries.filings_evidence: 10-K explicitly states: 'infrastructure investments in artificial intelligence ("AI") have increased substantially, which is driving significant demand increases in the Data Center Computing market. We accelerated investments to increase capacity and make upgrades to support higher demand and new product requirements in the market.' AEIS is a direct AI-capex beneficiary via power delivery to data centers and semi fabs. No language suggests coding agents or productivity software are a threat.
discoveries.thesis: AEIS's Risk Factors flag AI as a demand TAILWIND, not a threat. A Claude coding-agent product doesn't compete with power conversion for fabs or data centers — the opposite: agentic workloads driving inference demand require more of exactly what AEIS sells. The five-day drawdown has made this cheaper still.
discoveries.what_confirms: Hyperscaler capex commentary reaffirming, semi-cap peer stabilization, no AEIS-specific negative news in the window.
discoveries.what_kills: A specific AEIS customer (Applied Materials, LAM) issues a capex cut, a large data-center hyperscaler pauses buildout, or an AEIS 8-K reveals an operational issue.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Power-delivery infrastructure names get sympathy-tagged with generic 'tech selloff' even when their own filings explicitly cite the trigger event's category (AI) as a growth driver. Filings-language check is the fastest disconfirmation.
discoveries.catalyst: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: AEIS is loosely 'tech / semi-cap adjacent' and was swept into the same basket as ONTO/AMBA in a broad tech-tape decline.
research_pointers: Verify hyperscaler capex commentary from most recent AMZN/MSFT/GOOG earnings hasn't changed
Check AEIS's most recent Data Center Computing segment growth commentary
Look for any Section 232 semi tariff developments that could be the real (non-sympathy) driver

discoveries.name: Photronics, Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: Risk Factors describe Photronics as a photomask supplier to IC and FPD manufacturers. AI is not mentioned as a competitive threat anywhere in the risk factors. The core risks are microelectronics cyclicality, customer concentration, China trade risk, and equipment/materials supply — none of which are affected by a coding-agent product.
discoveries.thesis: Every chip that exists — including every accelerator that runs Claude Cowork inference — requires photomasks. There's no mechanism by which an application-layer AI agent reduces mask demand; it increases it. Panic on this news is mechanically backwards. Curation hypothesis confirmed by filings.
discoveries.what_confirms: IC design activity metrics remain strong (mask demand is driven by design releases, not just wafer volume, per filings); China semi buildout continues.
discoveries.what_kills: A specific customer-concentration event (top-2 customers are 29% of revenue) — if one of them cuts orders. Or an escalation of China export controls hitting Photronics's two China fabs directly.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Photomask demand is driven by DESIGN RELEASES not volume — this makes AI proliferation (more custom silicon designs from more players) a structural tailwind, not just a cyclical one. The filings say this explicitly.
discoveries.catalyst: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: Semi-cap sympathy tagging on broad tech-tape weakness; no product-level connection to coding agents.
research_pointers: Check whether the 5-day drawdown was Photronics-specific or sector-wide
Review recent PLAB quarterly design-release commentary
Cross-check with China semi export-control news which could be a real (non-sympathy) driver

discoveries.name: Cognex Corporation
discoveries.filings_evidence: Cognex explicitly flags AI as a competitive risk: 'Advancements in the availability of sophisticated AI algorithms and open-source machine vision platforms may lower barriers to enter our market... large technology companies and other competitors with substantial financial and technological resources may continue to develop and distribute free or low-cost solutions.' However, Cognex ALSO frames AI as a product opportunity ('continuing to introduce products embedded with AI technology that augments rule-based machine vision'). The threat language is general and predates this specific catalyst; there is no language tying industrial machine vision to Claude-style coding/productivity agents.
discoveries.thesis: Cognex has real, filings-acknowledged AI competition — but it's from open-source machine-vision models on factory floors, NOT from a coding agent for office productivity. Claude Cowork does not do deterministic sub-millisecond machine vision on a QA line. The panic is thematic, applying a general 'AI eats software' narrative to a very specific factory-floor workflow. The filings acknowledge the general threat but the trigger event itself does nothing to accelerate it.
discoveries.what_confirms: Peer industrial-automation names (KEYS, TER, ISRG-adjacent) trading flat; no customer-specific loss disclosures; Cognex continues rolling out its own AI-embedded vision products.
discoveries.what_kills: A major factory-automation customer (auto OEM, EV battery, life sciences) publicly switching to a general-purpose AI vision solution; or an open-source model demonstrating deterministic latency parity in an industrial-grade setting.
discoveries.what_to_learn: There's a big gap between 'LLM-vision demos' and factory-floor deterministic vision with certification, integrator networks, and lens/lighting hardware. Sympathy fades tend to collapse that gap.
discoveries.catalyst: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: Cognex is a machine-vision / AI-adjacent name that trades in sympathy with broad AI-software narratives, though its actual end market (industrial QA) is orthogonal to coding agents.
research_pointers: Check Moritex (optical components) integration progress — the moat is increasingly hardware+software combined
Watch for large-customer disclosures around alternative vision solutions
Note: filings do acknowledge AI competition risk — this is 'indirect' not 'none'

skipped.reason: Filings show AMBA is a direct AI edge-inference beneficiary (SoCs support VLMs, LLMs, VLAs, reasoning models on edge devices) — however the 5-day return is POSITIVE (+7.18%), so today's move looks more like profit-taking/tech-tape sympathy after a strong week rather than a fresh oversold panic setup. Not the cleanest overdone setup.
Moved UP today (+4.46%) — not a sympathy-fade victim; nothing to fade.
Moved UP today (+4.32%) — not a sympathy-fade victim; if anything a positive-day beneficiary.

Filings reveal REAL exposure — significant partner concentration risk, Performance Suite margin pressure from oncology cost trends already realized in 2024-2025, OBBBA Medicaid policy risk, and stock is already at $5.48 (broken chart, not clean sympathy fade). The curation false-victim hypothesis is contradicted by filings showing operational pressures beyond AI narrative. Small move (-4.53%) doesn't clear the panic-zone bar for a confident OVERDONE call.

_trigger.headline: Claude Cowork expands to mobile and web

This extends the reach of Anthropic's agent capabilities beyond the initial desktop-only release.
